I want to ask you one last thing if you have a second, which is tell me a little bit about using AI for story because I love the way you do that. And I know story is a very important part of storytelling for writers, for anyone really, the ability to tell stories, to incorporate stories into presentations, into talking to people. How do you use AI for that? What are the best practices for that? Yeah, ask for, and I'll just summarize this, the problems are a bit more detailed, but the problems are basically for you to ask for first person stories, second person stories, ask for moments of tension, like conflict. So for example, you would describe, you know, husband and wife are sitting down in the living room. So this is the prompt, husband and wife are sitting down in the living room on the couch after a long day for both of them. They're looking over their bills and they're wondering how to pay their bills. And then you click enter to get the output and it'll give you a scene that describes the husband and wife sitting on the couch and the dialogue they have. So you ask it for conflict, you give it stage direction, you ask for dialogue, you ask for what would they say, you ask for like, give me a plot twist. Like I could have given that exact scene and just ended and then give me a plot twist. And then it'll start the scene with them sitting on the couch talking about the bills and wondering how to pay it. And then it'll introduce a plot twist, whatever that might be, into the scene. And then that scene will take a turn. So think of it as you would write any story and just know you don't have to have a clear sense of the scene you're looking for or the plot, but just enough so that it has something to work with. So you describe that protagonist antagonist, you describe, you say I want a dialogue, or you say I want a descriptive scene and I want ABC to happen, you keep it generic, then it'll give you very specific outworking of what you're looking for in terms of plot, conflict, resolution or not, dialogue, perspectives. And like, and you can say, write it like a thriller novel, you can say write it like a romance novel, any kind of story framework you can imagine or find, which are endless, you can use all of that to give it story, scenes, plots, outlines for stories, dialogue, plot twist, all of it, all the story elements that exist in the world can be used to get stories from AI. It seems like we always come back to that analogy of like working with a junior writer, where you don't just say to a junior writer, write me a video sales letter, write me a 40 page sales letter, you say, okay, give me some headlines, you say, give me an introduction, you say, give me this, they go find out what the fears are for this audience because you can't expect them to do that. Yep, exactly. A question for you, does AI understand, like if you said, write me a hero's journey type story, would it be able to do that? Do you have to feed that in in order for it to do it? You can write me, write me a story, what you do, you don't, don't ask for the full story, because the quality will degrade, but you can ask for an outline. And you can say outline, outline a story about, you know, Bob Avatar, who's, who's doing XYZ. And then you say, write it, following the hero's journey framework. And then it'll give you an outline or a summary of a story based that follows the hero's journey framework. And then apply that to any known public publicly known framework of the past whatever 50 years, or even longer. And it'll know what those are, and it'll give you the output based on the plot framework that you want. Can it take, can you say, write me an Oh, Henry type story, and it'll write one of those pithy little Henry kind of stories. Write me a Hemingway, write me a that any style of like publicly known author. It'll write it in that style or type. That's a great tool for, you know, for, and again, it's always a matter of stimulating your own brain. It's not like you want to take that and then paste it into whatever you're working on or tell that exact story. If you're giving a speech. But you can sometimes you just want, Oh, that's an example of something that I could do. And then you start thinking of other things or you say, Oh, there's an element in sometimes there's one piece of and I think I've heard you say this even sometimes it's one little gold nugget. That's where it's in there that you wouldn't have thought of one phrase one sentence I asked for 20 creative ideas and like an idea number 15 the second sentence, express is something they go, that's really good. Yeah. Because you see it and you if you and this is why you need to like, don't replace your thinking or imagination with AI use it to enhance your thinking and augment your thinking. Because the output you get, you're not going to get quality, like excellent quality output every single time. It doesn't work that way. Just like you and I, like we sit down to write, like, we write 2000 words in a day, maybe only 100 of those words are actually good words. Like it's just, we, we nothing, we don't, you know, we don't wake up and piss excellence in the morning. We write crappy things. And sometimes you write for a couple of days, you realize this is just pure dog crap. I can't use it. And so you have to scrap it. Same thing. But then you look at it and you go, hmm, that sentence in that paragraph, I'm going to keep that because that's a nugget or a kernel of an idea or something that I can then use later. Same thing here. Yeah. Well, this has been great. I really appreciate this very much. Eye opening as always. Good, good, good. Thanks for having me. All right. You take care. Take care. All right.
